title: Addiction and ADHD
author: Caroline

seo.title: What is ADHD & How do ADHD and addiction overlap
seo.description: What is ADHD? & How do ADHD and addiction overlap? Call Plan Your Recovery St Louis Addiction CounselingTo schedule an in-person or telehealth consultation, call or text us as 314-467-8393

body:
People with ADHD sometimes feel like they always need to move and are "driven by a motor"

What is ADHD?

A person with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder displays a pattern of inattention and/or hyperactivity and impulsivity that interferes with everyday functioning.

Symptoms may include:

  • Having to re-read a sentence several times to grasp its meaning
  • Getting up and wandering around the office at work
  • Forgetting to complete household chores
  • Blurting out answers and interrupting others during conversations
  • Procrastination
  • Feeling overwhelmed when asked to make a decision.
  • Frequent lateness
  • Getting caught up in small, sometimes inconsequential, details.
  • Talking and fidgeting in times that others see as inappropriate.

Individuals with ADHD also may engage in a spectrum of risky but highly stimulating behaviors, such as:

  • Speeding or driving distracted
  • Impulsive shopping
  • Extreme sports
  • Making important decisions on a whim

How do ADHD and addiction overlap?

According to the National Comorbidity Study (NCS), people with ADHD are:

  • 1.8 times more likely to develop a nicotine use disorder
  • 1.8 times more likely to develop an alcohol use disorder
  • 5.2 times more likely to develop an illicit substance use disorder

(compared to people who do not have ADHD)

Stimulants and ADHD

Every substance that people misuse is dopaminergic at some level. Dopamine is connected to both focus and enjoyment. Increasing dopamine can help people with ADHD feel like they can focus.

Stimulant medications increase dompamine activation, helping patients to focus. They are the most effective treatment for ADHD. But in persons with a vulnerability to addiction they can sometimes lead to a stimulant use disorder.

Prescription stimulant medications can help people with ADHD perform previously difficult tasks, like sitting and reading.

Persons prescribe stimulants rarely misuse their own medication. This is because many people with ADHD who use stimulants to manage their condition dislike their side effects.

Stimulants may dampen the individual's normally lively personality. The individual is left less excitable and impulsive, making them feel unlike their usual self. People who are prescribed stimulant medication for ADHD often use it as little as possible, due to these side effects.

However, some individuals with ADHD do misuse their prescribed stimulant medications or acquire the medications from others. They may be fooled into thinking they cannot have too much of a good thing, only to be trapped into a cycle of addiction.

People with ADHD sometimes feel as though their mind is faceted and going a million different directions.

Impulsivity and substance use

People who have ADHD seek out experiences that will hold their attention. They’re also less likely to accurately perceive the risks that could occur from these behaviors. The combination of these traits leads individuals with ADHD to be more likely to misuse substances.

To illustrate: A young man with ADHD goes to a party. He drinks and has a good time. A few hours into the party, a friend pulls him aside and tells him that she has cocaine, and is willing to share. The young man perceives this as an exciting opportunity to experiment with a new, perhaps difficult to acquire, substance. Not wanting to miss out on an exciting experience, he says yes without considering the consequences. After using, the young man quickly notices the exhilaration of the experience. His attention is fully captured. He finds the experience highly pleasurable, and continues to connect with this friend to use further.

Once substance misuse enters the behavioral repertoire of a person with ADHD, it can be difficult to eliminate. As described above, impulsivity and sensation-seeking are a part of ADHD. Even after they have been sober for some time, a person with ADHD may give into an impulse to use. They may feel unable to withstand the temptation any longer, or they may be seeking comfort and distraction from the overwhelming nature of ADHD.

People with ADHD often struggle with impulsivity. This can show up in many different ways, from distracted driving to substance use.

Treatment

People with ADHD benefit from structure. Structure reduces impulsive decision making, and helps the individual create schedules that include movement and breaks from work.

This structure can be created through:

  • Mutual support groups, such as AA or moderation management.
  • Gaining stimulating and hands-on employment.
  • Engaging in activities that are simulating but not addictive. These activities can range from creative, low-risk hobbies to partaking in a high-contact sport.
  • Skills training in cognitive behavioral therapy

For individuals with ADHD who have misused medications in the past, there are also some non stimulant medications that can help. Two prominent examples include Wellbutrin and Strattera. A physician, preferably a psychiatrist, should be consulted to determine the best medication regimen for a person with ADHD.
